title: Check Palindrome String
description:
Given a string, check if it is a palindrome (reads same forwards and backwards).
Ignore case while checking. Print YES or NO.

Input: A single string
hints: Convert to lowercase first.
Compare s with its reverse.

solutions.python:
s = input().strip().lower()
print('YES' if s == s[::-1] else 'NO')

starterCode.python:
s = input().strip().lower()
# Compare s with its reverse
starterCode.java:
import java.util.*;
public class Main{ public static void main(String[] args){ String s=new Scanner(System.in).next(); } }
starterCode.c:
#include <stdio.h>
int main(){ char s[100005]; scanf("%s",s); return 0; }

explanation: Convert to lowercase, then compare string with its reverse. Two-pointer approach also works: compare s[i] with s[n-1-i].

title: Count Vowels in a String
description:
Count the number of vowels (a, e, i, o, u — both upper and lower case) in the input string.

Input: A single string
hints: Check each character against the set {a,e,i,o,u,A,E,I,O,U}.

solutions.python:
s = input()
print(sum(1 for c in s if c in 'aeiouAEIOU'))

starterCode.java:
import java.util.*;
public class Main{ public static void main(String[] args){ String s=new Scanner(System.in).nextLine(); } }
starterCode.c:
#include <stdio.h>
int main(){ char s[100005]; fgets(s,sizeof(s),stdin); return 0; }

explanation: Iterate over each character and check if it belongs to {a,e,i,o,u,A,E,I,O,U}. Maintain a counter.

title: String Compression
description:
Implement basic string compression: consecutive repeated characters are replaced by character + count.
If compressed string is not smaller, return original string.

Input: A string (only lowercase letters)
Example: "aabcccdddd" → "a2b1c3d4"
hints: Traverse string, count consecutive characters.
Append char+count. Check if result is shorter.

testCases.explanation: Compressed 'a1b1c1' is longer, so return original.

solutions.python:
s = input().strip()
if not s:
    print(s)
else:
    res, cnt = [], 1
    for i in range(1, len(s)):
        if s[i] == s[i-1]: cnt += 1
        else: res.append(s[i-1]+str(cnt)); cnt=1
    res.append(s[-1]+str(cnt))
    compressed = ''.join(res)
    print(compressed if len(compressed) < len(s) else s)

starterCode.java:
import java.util.*;
public class Main{ public static void main(String[] args){ String s=new Scanner(System.in).next(); } }
starterCode.c:
#include <stdio.h>
int main(){ char s[10005]; scanf("%s",s); return 0; }

explanation: Scan through characters, counting consecutive runs. Build compressed string. If compressed is shorter, return it; otherwise return original.

title: Longest Common Prefix
description:
Find the longest common prefix among N strings.
If none exists, print an empty line.

Input:
Line 1: N
Lines 2..N+1: One string per line
hints: Start with first string as prefix.
Shrink prefix until all strings start with it.

solutions.python:
n = int(input())
words = [input() for _ in range(n)]
pre = words[0]
for w in words[1:]:
    while not w.startswith(pre):
        pre = pre[:-1]
print(pre)

starterCode.python:
n = int(input())
words = [input() for _ in range(n)]
pre = words[0]
# Shrink prefix until all words start with it
starterCode.java:
import java.util.*;
public class Main{ public static void main(String[] args){ Scanner sc=new Scanner(System.in); int n=sc.nextInt(); sc.nextLine(); } }
starterCode.c:
#include <stdio.h>
int main(){ int n; scanf("%d ",&n); return 0; }

explanation: Take the first word as the initial prefix. Compare with each subsequent word and trim the prefix from the right until the word starts with it.
timeComplexity: O(S) where S = sum of all string lengths

title: First Unique Character
description:
Given a string, find the index (0-based) of the first non-repeating character.
If none exists, print -1.

Input: A single string of lowercase English letters
hints: Count frequency of each character.
Scan string again; first char with frequency 1 is the answer.

starterCode.python:
from collections import Counter
s = input().strip()
freq = Counter(s)
# Find first char with freq == 1
starterCode.java:
import java.util.*;
public class Main{ public static void main(String[] args){ String s=new Scanner(System.in).next(); } }
starterCode.c:
#include <stdio.h>
int main(){ char s[100005]; scanf("%s",s); return 0; }

explanation: Two-pass: first count frequencies, then find the first character whose count is exactly 1.

title: Reverse a Linked List
description:
Simulate a linked list using an array. Reverse the list and print elements space-separated.

Input:
Line 1: N (number of nodes)
Line 2: N space-separated values
hints: Three-pointer reversal: prev, curr, next.
Or simply reverse the array in-place.

solutions.python:
n = int(input())
arr = list(map(int, input().split()))
print(*arr[::-1])

starterCode.python:
n = int(input())
arr = list(map(int, input().split()))
# Reverse linked list (array-simulated)
starterCode.java:
import java.util.*;
public class Main{ public static void main(String[] args){ Scanner sc=new Scanner(System.in); int n=sc.nextInt(); int[] a=new int[n]; for(int i=0;i<n;i++) a[i]=sc.nextInt(); } }
starterCode.c:
#include <stdio.h>
int main(){ int n; scanf("%d",&n); return 0; }

explanation: Three-pointer technique: prev=null, curr=head, next=curr.next. In each step: save next, point curr.next to prev, advance prev and curr.

title: Find Middle of Linked List
description:
Find the middle node value of a linked list (array-simulated).
If the list has even length, return the second middle element.

Input:
Line 1: N
Line 2: N space-separated values
hints: Fast & slow pointer: fast moves 2 steps, slow moves 1.
When fast reaches end, slow is at middle.
Or simply return arr[N/2].

solutions.python:
n = int(input())
arr = list(map(int, input().split()))
print(arr[n // 2])

starterCode.python:
n = int(input())
arr = list(map(int, input().split()))
# Return arr[n//2] (second middle for even length)
starterCode.java:
import java.util.*;
public class Main{ public static void main(String[] args){ Scanner sc=new Scanner(System.in); int n=sc.nextInt(); int[] a=new int[n]; for(int i=0;i<n;i++) a[i]=sc.nextInt(); } }
starterCode.c:
#include <stdio.h>
int main(){ int n; scanf("%d",&n); return 0; }

explanation: Use the slow/fast pointer technique. Slow advances 1 step, fast advances 2. When fast hits the end, slow is at the middle. For array: middle = arr[N/2].

title: Detect Cycle in Linked List
description:
Given an array of next-pointers (0-indexed, -1 means null), determine if the linked list has a cycle.
Print YES if cycle exists, NO otherwise.

Input:
Line 1: N and head index
Line 2: N next-pointers (the i-th value is the next index for node i; -1 means null)
constraints:
1 <= N <= 10^4
head is always a valid index
hints: Floyd's cycle detection: slow moves 1, fast moves 2.
If fast or fast.next is null, no cycle.
If slow == fast at any point, cycle exists.

solutions.python:
line1 = input().split()
n, head = int(line1[0]), int(line1[1])
nxt = list(map(int, input().split()))
slow, fast = head, head
while True:
    if fast == -1 or (fast < n and nxt[fast] == -1): print('NO'); break
    slow = nxt[slow]
    fast = nxt[nxt[fast]]
    if slow == fast: print('YES'); break

starterCode.python:
line1 = input().split()
n, head = int(line1[0]), int(line1[1])
nxt = list(map(int, input().split()))
slow = fast = head
# Floyd's cycle detection
starterCode.java:
import java.util.*;
public class Main{ public static void main(String[] args){ Scanner sc=new Scanner(System.in); int n=sc.nextInt(),head=sc.nextInt(); int[] nxt=new int[n]; for(int i=0;i<n;i++) nxt[i]=sc.nextInt(); } }
starterCode.c:
#include <stdio.h>
int main(){ int n,head; scanf("%d %d",&n,&head); return 0; }

explanation: Floyd's Cycle Detection (Tortoise & Hare): slow moves 1 step, fast moves 2. If they ever meet, there's a cycle. If fast reaches null, no cycle.

title: Next Greater Element
description:
For each element in the array, find the next greater element (the first element to the right that is greater).
If no such element exists, output -1.

Input:
Line 1: N
Line 2: N integers
hints: Use a stack to keep track of elements waiting for their NGE.
Traverse left to right; when current element > stack top, pop and record.

solutions.python:
import sys
n = int(input())
arr = list(map(int, input().split()))
ans = [-1]*n
stack = []
for i in range(n):
    while stack and arr[stack[-1]] < arr[i]:
        ans[stack.pop()] = arr[i]
    stack.append(i)
print(*ans)

starterCode.python:
n = int(input())
arr = list(map(int, input().split()))
ans = [-1]*n
stack = []
# Monotonic stack approach
starterCode.java:
import java.util.*;
public class Main{ public static void main(String[] args){ Scanner sc=new Scanner(System.in); int n=sc.nextInt(); int[] a=new int[n]; for(int i=0;i<n;i++) a[i]=sc.nextInt(); } }
starterCode.c:
#include <stdio.h>
int main(){ int n; scanf("%d",&n); return 0; }

explanation: Monotonic stack: push indices. When arr[i] > arr[stack.top()], pop and set ans[popped] = arr[i]. This gives O(N) solution where each element is pushed and popped at most once.

title: Tower of Hanoi
description:
Print the sequence of moves to solve the Tower of Hanoi problem with N disks.
Move disks from rod 'A' to rod 'C' using rod 'B' as auxiliary.
Each move is printed as: "Move disk X from rod Y to rod Z"

Input: N (number of disks)
hints: Base case: Move disk 1 directly from source to destination.
Recursive step: Move N-1 disks to auxiliary, move disk N, then move N-1 from auxiliary to destination.

testCases.output:
Move disk 1 from rod A to rod B
Move disk 2 from rod A to rod C
Move disk 1 from rod B to rod C
Move disk 1 from rod A to rod C

solutions.python:
def hanoi(n, src, aux, dst):
    if n == 1:
        print(f'Move disk 1 from rod {src} to rod {dst}')
        return
    hanoi(n-1, src, dst, aux)
    print(f'Move disk {n} from rod {src} to rod {dst}')
    hanoi(n-1, aux, src, dst)
n = int(input())
hanoi(n, 'A', 'B', 'C')

starterCode.python:
def hanoi(n, src, aux, dst):
    # Base case: n == 1
    # Recursive case: move n-1, move n, move n-1
    pass
n = int(input())
hanoi(n, 'A', 'B', 'C')

explanation: Recursive solution: To move N disks from A to C — move N-1 from A to B (recursively), move disk N from A to C, move N-1 from B to C (recursively). Total moves = 2^N - 1.

title: Power Function (Fast Exponentiation)
description:
Compute base^exp (mod 10^9+7) efficiently using recursive fast exponentiation.

Input:
Line 1: base exp
hints: If exp is even: power(base, exp) = power(base*base, exp/2)
If exp is odd: base * power(base, exp-1)
This gives O(log exp) instead of O(exp).

solutions.python:
MOD = 10**9 + 7
base, exp = map(int, input().split())
def power(b, e):
    if e == 0: return 1
    if e % 2 == 0: return power(b*b % MOD, e//2)
    return b * power(b, e-1) % MOD
print(power(base, exp))

starterCode.python:
MOD = 10**9 + 7
base, exp = map(int, input().split())
def power(b, e):
    # Base case: e == 0
    # Even: power(b*b, e//2)
    # Odd: b * power(b, e-1)
    pass
print(power(base, exp))

explanation: Binary exponentiation: split exponent in half at each step. power(b, e) = power(b², e/2) if e even, else b × power(b, e-1). Reduces O(exp) to O(log exp).

title: Binary Search
description:
Given a sorted array of N integers and a target value, return the index of the target.
If not found, print -1.

Input:
Line 1: N and target
Line 2: N sorted integers
hints: mid = (left + right) / 2
If arr[mid] == target, return mid.
If arr[mid] < target, search right half; else search left half.

solutions.python:
line = input().split()
n, target = int(line[0]), int(line[1])
arr = list(map(int, input().split()))
lo, hi = 0, n - 1
ans = -1
while lo <= hi:
    mid = (lo + hi) // 2
    if arr[mid] == target: ans = mid; break
    elif arr[mid] < target: lo = mid + 1
    else: hi = mid - 1
print(ans)

starterCode.python:
line = input().split()
n, target = int(line[0]), int(line[1])
arr = list(map(int, input().split()))
lo, hi = 0, n - 1
# Binary search loop
starterCode.java:
import java.util.*;
public class Main{ public static void main(String[] args){ Scanner sc=new Scanner(System.in); int n=sc.nextInt(),target=sc.nextInt(); int[] a=new int[n]; for(int i=0;i<n;i++) a[i]=sc.nextInt(); } }
starterCode.c:
#include <stdio.h>
int main(){ int n,target; scanf("%d %d",&n,&target); return 0; }

explanation: Binary search halves the search space each iteration. Compare middle element with target; adjust left or right boundary accordingly. Runs in O(log N).

title: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
description:
A sorted array was rotated at an unknown pivot. Find the index of target.
Return -1 if not found.

Input:
Line 1: N and target
Line 2: N integers
hints: One half is always sorted after a rotation.
Determine which half is sorted, check if target lies in it, then narrow search.

solutions.python:
line = input().split()
n, target = int(line[0]), int(line[1])
arr = list(map(int, input().split()))
lo, hi = 0, n-1
while lo <= hi:
    mid = (lo+hi)//2
    if arr[mid] == target: print(mid); break
    if arr[lo] <= arr[mid]:  # left half sorted
        if arr[lo] <= target < arr[mid]: hi = mid - 1
        else: lo = mid + 1
    else:  # right half sorted
        if arr[mid] < target <= arr[hi]: lo = mid + 1
        else: hi = mid - 1
else:
    print(-1)

starterCode.python:
line = input().split()
n, target = int(line[0]), int(line[1])
arr = list(map(int, input().split()))
lo, hi = 0, n-1
# Modified binary search
starterCode.java:
import java.util.*;
public class Main{ public static void main(String[] args){ Scanner sc=new Scanner(System.in); int n=sc.nextInt(),target=sc.nextInt(); int[] a=new int[n]; for(int i=0;i<n;i++) a[i]=sc.nextInt(); } }
starterCode.c:
#include <stdio.h>
int main(){ int n,target; scanf("%d %d",&n,&target); return 0; }

explanation: Modified binary search: at each step, at least one half is sorted. Identify which half is sorted, check if target is in that range, and binary search accordingly.
